reasoning: The finding is code-proven accurate. Lines 88-99 of class-sync.php read `wp_capabilities` via get_user_meta and transmit every active role (including 'administrator', 'editor', 'shop_manager') to the Omnisend API via add_custom_property('wordpress_roles', ...). The four trigger hooks are confirmed in class-omnisend-core-bootstrap.php lines 69, 73, 78, 80: user_register, wp_login, profile_update, and the per-minute cron all call Sync::identify_user_by_id() or Sync::sync_contacts() with no capability check and no role exclusion list. The only gate is lines 66-81: if both is_omnisend_woocommerce_plugin_active() AND is_omnisend_woocommerce_plugin_connected() return true, the hooks are skipped (the WooCommerce plugin handles sync instead). For standalone installs all four hooks are active. No attacker-controlled input is involved — this is automatic passive exfiltration on normal site events. Severity is low, not medium/high, because: (1) data flows to the site owner's own Omnisend account, not to an arbitrary third party or attacker; (2) this is intentional CRM enrichment behavior standard in marketing automation plugins; (3) the actual security impact is limited to the site owner's Omnisend account gaining role metadata. The real concern is a privacy/compliance gap (GDPR Article 13 transparency — end users are not notified their WordPress role is transmitted to a third-party SaaS), not a traditional exploitable vulnerability with an adversary-controlled sink. No sanitizer or redaction guard was missed; none exists.
